did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning, specifically ResNet50 and ResNet101V2 convolutional neural networks (CNNs), to classify dermoscopic images of skin cancer (benign vs. malignant/healthy) and obtain results including accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results using deep learning models (ResNet50 and ResNet101V2) to classify skin cancer images. It includes a 'Proposed Methodology' section detailing data preprocessing and model training, a 'Results and Discussion' section with specific performance metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score) and confusion matrices, and a 'Conclusion'. Although the abstract mentions an 'analytical overview', the content is a primary research study reporting new experimental findings, published in a conference proceedings (ICICNCT).

title: Autonomous Skin Cancer Categorisation using Modern Deep Learning Techniques: An Analysis
journal: 2025 International Conference on Intelligent Communication Networks and Computational Techniques (ICICNCT)
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of accurately classifying benign and malignant skin lesions using modern deep learning techniques to improve diagnostic reliability. The authors utilized dermoscopic images from a dataset sourced from Kaggle, preprocessing them by resizing to 150x150 pixels and normalizing pixel values. Two ResNet architectures, ResNet50 and ResNet101V2, were employed for binary classification of healthy versus benign/malignant conditions after training on ImageNet weights. The models were evaluated using metrics including accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and ROC curves, with data split into 80% training, 10% validation, and 10% testing sets. The ResNet101V2 model achieved the highest performance with 97% accuracy, 0.92 sensitivity, and an AUC of 1.00, outperforming ResNet50 which achieved 93% accuracy and an AUC of 0.9849. The results demonstrate that deep learning models, particularly ResNet101V2, have significant potential for reliable and scalable skin cancer categorization.

lesion_types_studied.details: Used as one of the two classes in the binary classification task (healthy vs. benign). The study emphasizes the importance of classifying benign lesions to address class imbalance and improve diagnostic systems. Specific examples mentioned in the introduction include seborrhoeic keratoses, melanocytic nevi (moles), dermatofibromas, epidermoid cysts, lipomas, and benign adnexal tumours (syringomas, trichoepitheliomas), but the experimental dataset labels were aggregated into 'Benign'.

Used as the other class in the binary classification task. The dataset table labels this class as 'Normal' with 1200 images. The study aims to distinguish between healthy/normal skin and benign/malignant conditions.

The study focuses on 'skin cancer' generally, with specific mentions of melanoma, BCC, and SCC in the introduction as deadly types. However, the experimental binary classification task grouped malignant cases with benign cases against healthy/normal, or potentially the 'Benign' class in the table actually represented the positive class for 'skin cancer' detection depending on the specific model evaluation context (Table 1 lists 'Benign' and 'Normal', but the text discusses detecting 'skin cancer' and 'benign skin cancer'). The results section discusses detecting 'benign skin cancer' and distinguishing 'healthy and sick photos'. The confusion matrix for ResNet101V2 shows 219 TP and 20 FN for 'skin cancer', implying the positive class was skin cancer (likely the 'Benign' label in Table 1 was used to represent the pathological class, or the dataset contained benign lesions and the task was benign vs normal, but the text conflates 'benign skin cancer' and 'skin cancer'). Given the explicit mention of 'Benign' and 'Normal' in Table 1 and the binary nature, the studied entities are Benign Lesions and Normal Skin. Malignant types are mentioned as context but not explicitly separated in the experimental labels shown in Table 1.

all_task_types.target: Binary diagnostic category (Healthy/Normal vs. Benign/Malignant skin cancer)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the task is 'binary classification' to distinguish between 'healthy and benign circumstances' (or 'healthy and sick photos'). The models (ResNet50 and ResNet101V2) were modified for binary classification using sigmoid activation functions. The dataset consisted of two classes: 'Normal' (1200 images) and 'Benign' (1200 images). Performance was evaluated using classification metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall (sensitivity), specificity, F1-score, and ROC curves.

datasets_used.name: Kaggle Dermoscopic Image Dataset
datasets_used.description: A public dataset sourced from Kaggle containing dermoscopic images of skin lesions. The images were preprocessed by resizing to 150x150 pixels and normalizing pixel values to a range of 0 to 1.

use.training: Used for training the ResNet50 and ResNet101V2 models (80% of the data). Data augmentation techniques like flipping, zooming, and rotating were applied to the training set.
use.validation: Used for validation during the training process (10% of the data) to monitor convergence and loss.
use.testing: Used for final evaluation of model performance, including accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and confusion matrices (10% of the data).
use.other: The models were pre-trained on ImageNet weights before being fine-tuned on this dataset.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate classification performance by counting True Positives (TP), True Negatives (TN), False Positives (FP), and False Negatives (FN) for ResNet50 and ResNet101V2 models.
To measure the trade-off between the true positive rate (sensitivity) and the false positive rate (1-specificity) and to calculate the Area Under the Curve (AUC) for model comparison.

statistical_methods_used.method: Standard Classification Metrics (Accuracy, Precision, Recall/Sensitivity, Specificity, F1-score)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To quantitatively compare the performance of ResNet50 and ResNet101V2 models on the binary classification task.
To facilitate comprehensive analysis and prevent overfitting by separating data into distinct sets for training, validation, and final assessment.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and AUC, along with confusion matrices and ROC curves.

ResNet50.notes: The model effectively identifies cancer with the lowest FN rate. Its FP rate exceeds that of ResNet101V2, while its genuine negative rate is inferior. Specificity and sensitivity are not as superior as ResNet101V2 but remain commendable.

ResNet101.notes: Performance metrics listed in Table II. Specific confusion matrix or AUC values not explicitly detailed in text for this specific variant compared to ResNet101V2.

ResNet101V2.notes: Demonstrates efficacy in detecting benign skin cancer. It does a good job at telling the difference between photographs of normal and cancerous tissue. The model is exceptionally good at finding skin cancer since it can tell the difference between healthy and sick photos without making any mistakes (referring to FP=0). Highest identification accuracy among tested models.

Kaggle Dermoscopic Image Dataset.notes: ResNet101V2 achieved the best identification accuracy of 97%, outperforming ResNet50 which had 93%. The study utilized 50 training epochs. Data augmentation techniques included flipping, zooming, and rotating.

conclusions: ResNet101V2 achieved the highest performance among the tested models, with 97% accuracy, 0.92 sensitivity, 0.99 precision, and an AUC of 1.00, demonstrating exceptional efficacy in distinguishing between healthy and benign/malignant skin lesions.
ResNet50 achieved 93% accuracy and an AUC of 0.9849 but exhibited lower sensitivity (0.25 reported in text, though 0.97 in Table II) and a lower F1-score (0.4) compared to ResNet101V2.

The study highlights that contemporary AI models often prioritize malignant lesions while inadequately addressing benign illnesses, which complicates practical implementation and impairs the ability to distinguish between benign and malignant tumors.

Accurately classifying benign skin lesions is critical for mass screening, community-level teledermatology, and monitoring changes in benign lesions to prevent mismanagement and unnecessary treatments.

Deep learning models, particularly ResNet101V2, show significant potential for reliable, rapid, and scalable skin cancer categorization, potentially lowering false positives and reducing wasteful treatments.
The use of balanced datasets containing both benign and malignant lesions is essential for developing robust AI systems that can accurately sort patients and improve diagnostic completeness.
important_remarks: The study relies on a small, balanced dataset of only 2,400 dermoscopic images (1,200 Normal, 1200 Benign) sourced from Kaggle, which limits the generalizability of the results to broader clinical populations.

The binary classification task conflates 'Benign' lesions with 'Malignant' skin cancer in the positive class label (labeled 'Benign' in Table I but referred to as 'skin cancer' in results), creating ambiguity regarding the specific pathology being detected versus healthy skin.

The ResNet101V2 model reports an AUC of 1.00 and 0 False Positives on the test set, which is unusually high performance for medical imaging and may indicate overfitting or data leakage, especially given the small test set size (200 images).

There is a significant discrepancy in the reported Sensitivity for ResNet50: Table II lists it as 0.97, while the text in Section IV claims it is 0.25; the confusion matrix (217 TP, 5 FN) supports the higher value (~0.98), suggesting a reporting error in the text.

The F1-score for ResNet50 is reported as 0.40 in Table II, which is inconsistent with its high Precision (0.99) and Sensitivity (0.97); this suggests a potential calculation error or label mismatch in the evaluation metrics.
The paper does not report any statistical significance tests (e.g., p-values) to validate the performance differences between the models, relying solely on descriptive metrics.

The dataset lacks diversity in terms of skin types and lesion subtypes (aggregating all benign lesions into one class), which may bias the model's performance against underrepresented groups or specific benign pathologies.
No external validation was performed; the model was only evaluated on the same Kaggle dataset used for training and testing, raising concerns about real-world clinical applicability.
